questions.question: What is std::span?
options: Non-owning view over contiguous data — pointer + size, zero allocation
questions.correctAnswer: Non-owning view over contiguous data — pointer + size, zero allocation
questions.explanation: span stores T* and size_t. No ownership. No allocation.

questions.question: What's the difference between span<T> and span<T,N>?
options: span<T> = dynamic extent (16 bytes); span<T,N> = static extent (8 bytes, size in type)
questions.correctAnswer: span<T> = dynamic extent (16 bytes); span<T,N> = static extent (8 bytes, size in type)
questions.explanation: Static-extent spans store only the pointer — size is compile-time constant.

questions.question: Can span prevent buffer overruns?
options: In debug builds yes (bounds-checked access); in release it enables range-for which can't overrun
questions.correctAnswer: In debug builds yes (bounds-checked access); in release it enables range-for which can't overrun
questions.explanation: span enables debug hardening and range-based loops that naturally can't exceed bounds.

questions.question: What types can span be constructed from?
options: Vector, array, C array, pointer+size, other spans
questions.correctAnswer: Vector, array, C array, pointer+size, other spans
questions.explanation: span is universally constructible from any contiguous range.

questions.question: Does span::subspan() allocate memory?
options: No — just pointer arithmetic, O(1) zero-cost slicing
questions.correctAnswer: No — just pointer arithmetic, O(1) zero-cost slicing
questions.explanation: Like string_view, span::subspan adjusts pointer and length — no allocation, no copy.

questions.question: What does fs::create_directories() do?
questions.correctAnswer: Creates all intermediate directories (mkdir -p)
questions.explanation: create_directories creates the full path including any missing parent directories.

questions.question: What does path::stem() return for 'archive.tar.gz'?
questions.correctAnswer: archive.tar
questions.explanation: stem() removes only the last extension. extension() returns .gz.

questions.question: How do you iterate over files in a directory?
questions.correctAnswer: fs::directory_iterator
questions.explanation: directory_iterator iterates non-recursively. recursive_directory_iterator goes into subdirectories.

questions.question: What is the advantage of error_code overloads over exceptions?
options: No exceptions in game loops — error_code is predictable, no stack unwinding cost
questions.correctAnswer: No exceptions in game loops — error_code is predictable, no stack unwinding cost
questions.explanation: Game loops prefer error_code — filesystem errors are expected (file not found, permission denied).

questions.question: What encoding does fs::path use on Windows vs Linux?
questions.correctAnswer: Windows: UTF-16 (wchar_t); Linux: UTF-8 (char)
questions.explanation: path abstracts platform encoding. Use path's string conversions, not raw c_str().

questions.question: How does std::deque achieve O(1) push_front AND push_back?
options: Array of pointers to fixed-size blocks (chunked array)
questions.correctAnswer: Array of pointers to fixed-size blocks (chunked array)
questions.explanation: deque = chunked array. Push front allocates new block; push back same. No element shift.

questions.question: Which container adaptor is FIFO?
questions.correctAnswer: queue
questions.explanation: queue adapts deque to provide FIFO: push at back, pop from front.

questions.question: What internal structure does std::set use?
options: Red-black tree — same as std::map but keys only, no values
questions.correctAnswer: Red-black tree — same as std::map but keys only, no values
questions.explanation: std::set is a sorted container of unique keys. O(log n) operations.

questions.question: When should you use deque instead of vector?
options: When you need O(1) push_front AND push_back, and don't need contiguous memory
questions.correctAnswer: When you need O(1) push_front AND push_back, and don't need contiguous memory
questions.explanation: deque: O(1) both ends. vector: O(1) back only, O(n) front. deque elements NOT contiguous.

questions.question: What does std::priority_queue provide?
options: Always returns the largest element (max-heap by default)
questions.correctAnswer: Always returns the largest element (max-heap by default)
questions.explanation: priority_queue is a max-heap. top() returns the largest element. pop() removes it.

questions.question: What is the key difference between vector<int> and pmr::vector<int>?
options: pmr::vector uses runtime polymorphic allocator — same type regardless of memory source
questions.correctAnswer: pmr::vector uses runtime polymorphic allocator — same type regardless of memory source
questions.explanation: All pmr::vector<int> share the same type. Allocator is a runtime pointer, not template param.

questions.question: What is a monotonic_buffer_resource?
options: Arena allocator — bump-pointer, freed all at once on destruction
questions.correctAnswer: Arena allocator — bump-pointer, freed all at once on destruction
questions.explanation: monotonic_buffer_resource = bump allocator. No individual frees. Reset by destroying the resource.

questions.question: What is the performance advantage of pmr + monotonic over default allocator?
options: Allocations are bump-pointer (extremely fast); no fragmentation; no per-object heap overhead
questions.correctAnswer: Allocations are bump-pointer (extremely fast); no fragmentation; no per-object heap overhead
questions.explanation: Monotonic: one subtraction per alloc. No heap walking. Benchmark: 3-10x faster than default allocator.

questions.question: What does unsynchronized_pool_resource do?
options: Pool of same-sized blocks — not thread-safe, designed for single-thread use
questions.correctAnswer: Pool of same-sized blocks — not thread-safe, designed for single-thread use
questions.explanation: unsynchronized = no internal locking. Faster for single-thread use. Use synchronized_pool_resource for thread safety.

questions.question: How do you use std::pmr with existing code that takes const vector<int>&?
options: pmr::vector<int> IS the same type — works with any memory_resource*
questions.correctAnswer: pmr::vector<int> IS the same type — works with any memory_resource*
questions.explanation: The killer feature: same type regardless of allocator. Functions taking pmr::vector<T> work with any backing resource.

questions.question: What does virtual enable?
options: Runtime dispatch — function called depends on actual object type, not pointer type
questions.correctAnswer: Runtime dispatch — function called depends on actual object type, not pointer type
questions.explanation: 'virtual: Don't decide based on pointer type. Look at actual object at runtime.'

questions.question: What does override do?
options: Compiler verifies function actually overrides base virtual — catches typos and signature mismatches
questions.correctAnswer: Compiler verifies function actually overrides base virtual — catches typos and signature mismatches
questions.explanation: 'Always use override. Catches typos, wrong params, wrong const, missing virtual in base.'

questions.question: Why virtual destructor required?
options: Delete through base pointer calls correct derived destructor chain
questions.correctAnswer: Delete through base pointer calls correct derived destructor chain
questions.explanation: Without virtual destructor, delete Base* only calls ~Base(), leaking derived resources.

questions.question: What is object slicing?
options: Copying a derived object to a base object — derived members are sliced off
questions.correctAnswer: Copying a derived object to a base object — derived members are sliced off
questions.explanation: 'Enemy e; Character c = e; — SLICED! c is just Character. Enemy members lost.'

questions.question: What does final do on a class?
options: Prevents further inheritance — no class can derive from it
questions.correctAnswer: Prevents further inheritance — no class can derive from it
questions.explanation: final on class = no more derived classes. final on virtual function = no further overrides.

questions.question: What does the capture [] control?
options: Which external variables lambda can access and how (by value or reference)
questions.correctAnswer: Which external variables lambda can access and how (by value or reference)
questions.explanation: Capture list: [=] copies everything, [&] references everything, [x, &y] mixed.

questions.question: What is the danger of [&] in async lambdas?
options: Dangling references — if lambda outlives captured variables
questions.correctAnswer: Dangling references — if lambda outlives captured variables
questions.explanation: 'Capturing by Reference Is Dangerous. If referenced variable destroyed before lambda runs → UB.'

questions.question: What does mutable do on a lambda?
options: Allows modifying captured-by-value variables inside the lambda
questions.correctAnswer: Allows modifying captured-by-value variables inside the lambda
questions.explanation: By default, lambda operator() is const. mutable removes that const.

questions.question: What does std::function cost compared to a template parameter?
options: Virtual-like dispatch + potential heap allocation for large captures
questions.correctAnswer: Virtual-like dispatch + potential heap allocation for large captures
questions.explanation: std::function uses type erasure. Prefer template parameters for performance-critical code.

questions.question: What is a generic lambda (C++14)?
options: Lambda with auto parameters — generates templated operator()
questions.correctAnswer: Lambda with auto parameters — generates templated operator()
questions.explanation: [](auto a, auto b) { return a + b; } generates a template operator(). Works with any types.

questions.question: Which cast performs safe runtime-checked downcast?
questions.correctAnswer: dynamic_cast
questions.explanation: dynamic_cast walks class hierarchy at runtime. Returns nullptr (pointer) or throws (reference) on failure.

questions.question: What does static_cast do for downcasts?
options: No runtime check — compiles silently, UB if wrong type
questions.correctAnswer: No runtime check — compiles silently, UB if wrong type
questions.explanation: 'static_cast downcasts are dangerous. No runtime check — if Base* is actually Base, not Derived → UB.'

questions.question: What does const_cast remove?
questions.correctAnswer: const or volatile qualifiers
questions.explanation: const_cast is the ONLY cast that can change cv-qualifiers. Modifying a const-declared object is still UB.

questions.question: What does reinterpret_cast do?
options: Reinterprets bits of one type as another — no conversion, just treat these bytes as different type
questions.correctAnswer: Reinterprets bits of one type as another — no conversion, just treat these bytes as different type
questions.explanation: reinterpret_cast is the nuclear option. Only for serialization, memory-mapped I/O, opaque handles.

questions.question: What does C++20's std::bit_cast provide over reinterpret_cast?
options: Defined behavior for trivially copyable types — no strict aliasing violation
questions.correctAnswer: Defined behavior for trivially copyable types — no strict aliasing violation
questions.explanation: 'std::bit_cast guarantees same size, copies bits, doesn't violate strict aliasing. Always prefer over reinterpret_cast.'
